title: Per-context FIFO queue with concurrency limits

brief.why: Need sequential processing within a context (e.g. one email thread) but parallel processing across contexts. NanoClaw's GroupQueue pattern: per-group FIFO, global max of 3 concurrent agents, exponential backoff on failure.
brief.what: 1. Queue class: enqueue(contextId, triggerFn), drain(), shutdown() 2. Per-context ordering (FIFO within a context group) 3. Global concurrency limit (configurable, default 3) 4. Exponential backoff on spawn failure (5s base, max 5 retries) 5. Lane policy per context: followup (default), steer (interrupt), or collect (batch)
brief.done: Three triggers for different contexts process in parallel. Two triggers for the same context process sequentially. A spawn failure retries with backoff. Concurrency limit is respected.

brief.why: Agents spawned by the daemon need context that interactive sessions get from the user: they're unattended, should use mise for email ops, follow bon conventions, write handoffs, and know their escalation path (HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED → Gueridon notification). Without this, daemon-spawned agents will behave like confused interactive sessions.
brief.what: 1. Section in project CLAUDE.md: 'When spawned by the daemon' 2. Covers: you are unattended, draft-only default, escalation via HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED, handoff writing is mandatory, use mise for all Google Workspace ops 3. Role-specific append via Agent SDK systemPrompt.append (email triage, heartbeat, worker, reflector)
brief.done: A daemon-spawned agent reading CLAUDE.md knows it's unattended, knows its escalation path, and writes a handoff on exit without being told.

brief.why: The daemon needs to run persistently, survive reboots, and be manageable via standard OS tooling. NanoClaw ships both a launchd plist and systemd unit.
brief.what: 1. launchd plist for macOS (com.aboyeur.daemon.plist) 2. systemd unit for Linux (aboyeur-daemon.service) 3. Log rotation config 4. Graceful shutdown handler (SIGTERM drains queue, waits for active agents)
brief.done: launchctl load starts the daemon on macOS. systemctl --user start aboyeur starts it on Linux. Daemon survives reboots. Logs rotate.

title: The explore-decide-plan-execute cycle runs with minimal human gates

brief.why: Today the human (Sameer) steers the full cycle: commissions research, relays between Claudes, challenges assumptions, decides direction, transitions to planning. Most of this is mechanical or already covered by the reflector pattern. Encoding it means Aboyeur can run multi-session projects with human involvement only at irreversible decision points.
brief.what: 1. Explore-phase prompts (worker: research options; reflector: challenge assumptions and find gaps) 2. READY-TO-PLAN gate criteria (no unexplained data flows, irreversible decisions made by human, prior work surfaced) 3. Plan-phase prompts (worker: convert direction to Bons; reflector: check Bons against research) 4. Decision heuristics for unattended operation (reversible=proceed, irreversible/expensive=HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED) 5. Garde-manger integration for 'we explored this before' lookups
brief.done: Aboyeur can take a desired outcome, commission research across sessions, challenge its own assumptions, present decision points to human, convert approved direction to Bons, and begin execution — with human only needed for go/no-go on irreversible choices.

title: OAuth credentials for claude@planetmodha.com

brief.why: Need a separate Google Cloud OAuth client under planetmodha.com that the daemon uses to mint tokens for claude@planetmodha.com. This gives the daemon (and only the daemon's mise instance) access to that mailbox with send permissions. The ITV mise instance never gets send capability — enforced by separate OAuth clients, not just config.
brief.what: 1. Create OAuth client in planetmodha.com Google Cloud project 2. Configure with gmail.modify scope (includes send) 3. Daemon uses this client to mint/refresh tokens for claude@planetmodha.com 4. Store credentials separate from ITV OAuth client 5. Verify: daemon can list and send messages for claude@planetmodha.com
brief.done: jeton get-token --account claude@planetmodha.com returns a valid OAuth token with gmail.modify scope. Token refresh works unattended.

waiting_for: Sameer creates OAuth client in planetmodha.com Google Cloud project and runs initial token mint

title: Garde-manger integration for prior work discovery

brief.why: In this conversation, Sameer had to manually supply the earlier email infrastructure research. An explore reflector should search garde-manger for 'have we explored this before?' and surface relevant prior sessions, preventing re-discovery of settled questions.
brief.what: 1. Add garde-manger search step to explore-reflector prompt 2. Search patterns: topic keywords, repo names, tool names mentioned in the exploration 3. Surface relevant handoffs and session summaries as context 4. Reflector notes: 'Prior session on [date] concluded [X] — incorporate or revisit?'
brief.done: An explore-reflector reviewing a topic we've previously explored finds and cites the prior session. Prior conclusions are incorporated rather than re-derived.

brief.why: OpenClaw's best pattern: before context compaction, a silent turn fires where the agent saves critical state to external files. This prevents the gradual context erosion that degrades long-running agents. The Agent SDK supports PreCompact hooks.
brief.what: 1. PreCompact hook in Claude Code settings.json that triggers agent to write current state to handoff file 2. State includes: what was being worked on, what's done, what's blocked, key decisions made 3. Hook fires before CC's internal summarization 4. Handoff file written to standard location (~/.claude/handoffs/) 5. No Agent SDK required — CC hooks work for any spawned claude process
brief.done: An agent session that runs long enough to trigger compaction has its critical state preserved in a handoff file. A new session reading that handoff can continue without asking 'what was I doing?'

brief.why: Everything else depends on a clean spawn function. Gueridon's bridge.ts already does this: env-var stripping, stdio JSON streaming, session resume, orphan tracking. Extract that pattern into a reusable function rather than introducing the Agent SDK as a new dependency.
brief.what: 1. Extract spawnAgent(folder, prompt, sessionId?, options?) from Gueridon's spawnCC() pattern 2. Env-var stripping (CLAUDECODE + CLAUDE_CODE_ENTRYPOINT) 3. claude CLI with --input-format stream-json --output-format stream-json 4. Session resume via --session-id / --resume flags 5. Stdout event parsing (reuse Gueridon's event handling) 6. Configurable max_turns via --max-turns flag 7. Return type: {status, result, newSessionId}
brief.done: Can spawn a Claude session from a script, pass it a prompt, get structured output back. Session resume works across invocations. Process exits cleanly. No Agent SDK dependency — uses claude CLI directly.

steps: Extract spawnAgent(folder, prompt, sessionId?, options?) from Gueridon's spawnCC() pattern
Stdout event parsing (reuse Gueridon's event handling)

brief.why: Current reflector-open.md reviews code and work output. A planning reflector reviews architecture and design — challenging assumptions, walking through data flows, finding hidden decisions. This is what caught the 'mise polling needs a Claude' mistake.
brief.what: 1. New prompt file: shared/prompts/planning-reflector.md 2. Walk-through-every-data-flow section 3. Challenge-the-layering section 4. Find-hidden-decisions section 5. READY-TO-PLAN gate criteria 6. Garde-manger integration for 'we explored this before' lookups
brief.done: Planning reflector catches at least one non-obvious assumption error when run against the daemon's own architecture. The READY-TO-PLAN gate produces a clear summary of decisions made and risks remaining.

steps: Garde-manger integration for 'we explored this before' lookups

title: SQLite trigger table and polling loop

brief.why: Triggers from different sources (Gmail, cron, filesystem) need dedup, cursor tracking, and crash recovery. SQLite is the proven pattern — NanoClaw uses it, it survives process restarts, and it's queryable for debugging.
brief.what: 1. Schema: triggers(id, source, context_group, payload, status, created_at, claimed_at) 2. Polling loop (2s interval) drains pending triggers 3. Cursor tracking per source (last_seen timestamp) 4. Dedup by source+payload hash 5. Crash recovery: unclaimed triggers from before last startup get re-queued
brief.done: Triggers inserted by any source appear in the queue and get processed exactly once. After a crash, pending triggers are recovered on restart.

steps: Schema: triggers(id, source, context_group, payload, status, created_at, claimed_at)
Polling loop (2s interval) drains pending triggers
Crash recovery: unclaimed triggers from before last startup get re-queued

brief.why: When the human is unavailable, the Aboyeur Claude needs rules for when to proceed vs when to wait. Reversible and cheap = proceed. Irreversible or expensive = HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED. These heuristics encode the judgment calls the human would make.
brief.what: 1. Decision matrix in shared/prompts/decision-heuristics.md 2. Axes: reversibility (easy/hard/impossible), cost (cheap/moderate/expensive), novelty (proven pattern/new approach), blast radius (local/shared/public) 3. Rules: proceed if reversible+cheap+proven; escalate if any axis is red; default to escalate when uncertain 4. Integration point: reflector prompts reference the heuristics when evaluating 'should we proceed or ask?'
brief.done: An Aboyeur reflector facing a reversible cheap decision proceeds without human input. An Aboyeur reflector facing an irreversible decision writes HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED with the specific decision to be made. No false negatives on irreversible decisions in testing.

brief.why: The daemon's mise instance needs send capability for claude@planetmodha.com. But this codepath must NEVER exist in the ITV mise installation. Options: git branch that only the daemon deploys from, a feature flag gated by OAuth client identity, or a fork. The separation must be structural (code doesn't exist) not just config (code exists but is disabled).
brief.what: 1. New tool: tools/send.py wrapping gmail.users.drafts().send() 2. Add 'send' to OPERATIONS frozenset and _DISPATCH dict in server.py 3. This code lives ONLY in the daemon's mise deployment — not in the ITV branch 4. Git strategy: daemon branch of mise-en-space that carries send.py, rebased onto main. Or: mise loads tools from a plugin directory, send.py lives outside the repo. 5. Tests (in daemon branch only)
brief.done: mcp__mise__do(operation='send', draft_id='...') sends a draft. Without --allow-send config, the operation returns an error explaining it's disabled. Tests pass.

title: Upgrade conductor to Agent SDK spawning

brief.why: conductor.sh currently shells out to adapters. The conductor is a Claude-controlled process (the frontal lobe) — it makes decisions about what to do next. The daemon is just code (the lizard brain) — it reacts to triggers. They're separate: daemon spawns agents including the conductor Claude; the conductor Claude uses spawnAgent() to spawn workers and reflectors. No Agent SDK — uses Gueridon's proven claude CLI + stream-json pattern.
brief.what: 1. Replace adapter shell-out with spawnAgent() call (extracted from Gueridon's bridge.ts) 2. Pass worker-open.md or reflector-open.md via --append-system-prompt flag 3. Session resume between worker/reflector cycles (optional — fresh context may be better) 4. Structured handoff detection (parse stdout events for handoff file path) 5. Keep the conductor under 200 lines — complexity stays in prompts
brief.done: conductor.sh replaced by conductor.ts that alternates worker/reflector sessions via spawnAgent(). Same stuck detection (bon hash), same escalation (HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED). Workers and reflectors get the right prompt files.

title: Conductor routing: when one-shot vs when conductor session

brief.why: The daemon spawns agents for triggers, but not all triggers need the same treatment. A simple email triage is one-shot (spawn, process, exit). A multi-session coding project needs a conductor Claude that alternates workers and reflectors. The daemon needs a routing decision: which kind of spawn for which trigger? Without this, either everything gets an expensive conductor or nothing gets reflector review.
brief.what: 1. Trigger metadata includes complexity hint (one-shot vs orchestrated) 2. One-shot: daemon spawns agent directly with task prompt 3. Orchestrated: daemon spawns conductor Claude that manages worker/reflector cycles 4. Default: one-shot (email triage, heartbeat checks). Orchestrated only when explicitly configured per context or when the conductor itself is requested
brief.done: Email trigger spawns a one-shot agent. A bon-tracked project trigger spawns a conductor. The routing logic is clear in the daemon code and documented in CLAUDE.md.

title: Email triage prompt template and thread context

brief.why: The agent spawned by the Gmail trigger needs a prompt that orients it: who sent the email, what's the thread history, what actions are available (reply draft, escalate, archive, ignore). Thread context across sessions needs per-thread handoff files.
brief.what: 1. Prompt template: shared/prompts/email-triage.md 2. Classification categories: reply (draft), escalate (notify human), archive (no action), defer (add to reading queue) 3. Per-thread handoff file pattern: handoffs/email/{thread_id}.md 4. Thread context loading: agent reads thread handoff + fetches recent messages via mise 5. Escalation path: write HUMAN REVIEW NEEDED to handoff, daemon notifies via Gueridon push
brief.done: An email from a known contact gets a draft reply. An email requesting a decision gets escalated with a notification. A newsletter gets archived with a one-line summary in the thread handoff. All three paths tested.

title: Gmail poll trigger via direct API

brief.why: The daemon polls Gmail directly (not via mise MCP, which requires a Claude session). Jeton provides OAuth tokens. Gmail API returns message list with minimal overhead. Mise is for the agent's rich operations (fetch, draft, reply), not for the daemon's heartbeat check.
brief.what: 1. Gmail API client using jeton OAuth token 2. Poll: users.messages.list(q='is:unread newer_than:5m') every 60s 3. For each new message: insert trigger row (source=gmail, context_group=thread_id, payload={messageId, subject, from}) 4. Cursor tracking via Gmail historyId (more reliable than timestamp)
brief.done: New email to claude@planetmodha.com creates a trigger row within 60 seconds. Duplicate emails are not re-triggered. Token refresh works unattended.

title: Verify Agent SDK API shape

brief.why: Two actions (aby-vaniho context-pressure reflector, aby-hiwuca memory flush) depend on PreCompact hooks existing in the Agent SDK. NanoClaw uses them, which is evidence, but we haven't confirmed the API directly. A Claude session can npm install the SDK, read the types, and confirm query(), hooks, session resume, and the PreCompact interface — preventing rework if the API is different from what we assume.
brief.what: 1. npm init + npm install @anthropic-ai/claude-agent-sdk in a scratch dir 2. Read the TypeScript type definitions for query(), hooks, PreCompact, session resume 3. Confirm: PreCompact hook exists and receives transcript_path and session_id 4. Confirm: query() accepts resume and systemPrompt options 5. Document actual API shape in docs/agent-sdk-api.md 6. Flag any mismatches with our assumptions
brief.done: docs/agent-sdk-api.md exists with confirmed API shapes for query(), PreCompact hook, and session resume. Any assumption mismatches are flagged and corresponding bons are updated.

done_note: Unnecessary — Gueridon already provides the spawn primitive via claude CLI + stream-json. No SDK needed.

brief.why: No system has solved context compaction in long-running agents. Agent Teams fails catastrophically. The reflector pattern is the answer: when a worker nears compaction, spawn a reflector to summarize into a handoff, then start a fresh worker. This is compaction-as-review, not compaction-as-loss.
brief.what: 1. PreCompact hook in Claude Code settings.json (not SDK — CC hooks work for any claude process) 2. Hook writes a sentinel file that the daemon detects 3. Daemon spawns reflector with 'the previous worker is about to lose context — summarize and handoff' prompt 4. Reflector reads worker's partial handoff and recent tool output 5. Reflector writes full handoff with assessment, risks, and next steps 6. Daemon spawns fresh worker hydrated from that handoff
brief.done: A worker session that runs long enough to trigger compaction gets a reflector review. The next worker session picks up from the handoff with no context loss. Tested with a deliberately long-running task.

title: Worker Claudes get fresh-eyes review and context survives compaction

brief.why: Long-running agents lose context when compaction fires (Agent Teams loses team awareness entirely). Workers make mistakes that accumulate without review. The reflector pattern solves both — fresh eyes catch errors, and the handoff file preserves state across context boundaries.
brief.what: 1. Upgrade conductor.sh to spawn via Agent SDK (not shell adapter) 2. Add context-pressure trigger — spawn reflector when worker nears compaction limits 3. Reflector writes handoff that hydrates the next worker 4. Pre-compaction memory flush hook (agent saves critical state before summarization) 5. Planning reflector prompt for architecture review (not just code review)
brief.done: A worker session that runs for 45+ minutes gets a reflector review before context is lost. The next worker resumes seamlessly from the handoff. Planning-phase reflectors catch assumption errors like the 'mise polling needs a Claude' mistake.

brief.why: Periodic batched checks (check email, check bon progress, check calendar) should fire on a schedule. OpenClaw's HEARTBEAT.md pattern: a markdown checklist that a periodic agent processes in one turn. More efficient than separate triggers for each check.
brief.what: 1. Cron trigger source in daemon (configurable interval, default 30m) 2. Reads HEARTBEAT.md from project directory 3. Inserts trigger with payload=checklist content 4. Agent spawned with 'process this checklist, report only what needs attention' prompt
brief.done: A HEARTBEAT.md in a project directory gets processed every 30 minutes. Items that need no action produce no output. Items that need attention trigger notifications.

brief.why: Carlini's key insight: environment-as-prompt means tests matter more than prompt sophistication. The daemon's full cycle (trigger → queue → spawn → agent work → output → route) needs an automated test that doesn't require a real Gmail account or Max subscription. Mock the boundaries, test the plumbing.
brief.what: 1. Test SQLite trigger insertion and dedup 2. Test queue ordering and concurrency limits 3. Test spawnAgent with a mock Agent SDK (return canned output) 4. Test full cycle: mock trigger → queue → mock spawn → parse output → verify routing 5. Test crash recovery: kill daemon mid-cycle, restart, verify pending triggers are recovered
brief.done: npm test passes. Full cycle test runs in <5 seconds with no external dependencies. Crash recovery test verified.

brief.why: The explore→plan transition needs worker/reflector prompts for each phase. Explore workers research options; explore reflectors challenge assumptions. Plan workers convert direction to Bons; plan reflectors check Bons against research. These are the prompts that encode what Sameer did in this conversation.
brief.what: 1. shared/prompts/explore-worker.md — research, build options, sketch architectures 2. shared/prompts/explore-reflector.md — challenge assumptions, walk through data flows, find hidden decisions 3. shared/prompts/plan-worker.md — convert approved direction to Bon outcomes and actions 4. shared/prompts/plan-reflector.md — check Bons against research, verify completeness 5. Phase transition criteria in each reflector prompt
brief.done: Running explore-worker then explore-reflector on a new problem produces a clear options summary with challenged assumptions. Running plan-worker then plan-reflector produces well-formed Bons with all three flags populated.

title: Incoming email gets triaged and drafted without human involvement

brief.why: First concrete use case for the daemon. Validates the full stack: trigger detection, agent spawning, tool usage (mise), response routing. Email is high-value — saves daily time — and draft-only mode is safe.
brief.what: 1. OAuth credentials for claude@planetmodha.com via jeton 2. Add send operation to mise-en-space (locked behind explicit flag) 3. Email triage prompt template (classify, draft reply or escalate) 4. Gmail poll trigger in daemon (direct API, not via mise MCP) 5. Thread context via per-thread handoff files
brief.done: Email to claude@planetmodha.com gets a draft reply in Gmail within 5 minutes. Human reviews and sends. Escalation emails trigger a notification via Gueridon.

title: Triggers spawn Claude agents without human initiation

brief.why: Currently every Claude session requires a human to start it. For autonomous email triage, scheduled work, and event-driven responses, the system needs to react to external triggers (Gmail, cron, webhooks) and spawn agents independently.
brief.what: A thin persistent daemon (~700 lines Node/Python) that: 1. Polls external triggers via native APIs (not MCP) 2. Normalizes triggers into a SQLite queue 3. Drains the queue through a per-context FIFO with concurrency limits 4. Spawns Claude sessions via Agent SDK with Gueridon's env-var stripping pattern 5. Parses output markers and routes responses
brief.done: Daemon runs as a systemd/launchd service. A new email to claude@planetmodha.com results in a Claude session being spawned within 60 seconds, with no human involvement.
